q: A Structured Streaming job writes to a Delta table and your monitoring shows the file count growing unboundedly. What's happening and how do you fix it?
a: Each micro-batch appends a new set of Parquet files to the Delta table. With a 30-second trigger and small batch sizes, you accumulate 2,880+ files per day on a single table — every read now must open and scan hundreds of tiny files, causing metadata overhead and slow query times. There are three production fixes: (1) Set autoOptimize.optimizeWrite=true as a table property — Databricks dynamically coalesces files during the write itself, producing fewer larger files without extra overhead; (2) Set autoOptimize.autoCompact=true — Databricks runs a lightweight OPTIMIZE after each write, further merging small files in the background; (3) Schedule an explicit OPTIMIZE + ZORDER on a maintenance job (nightly, off-peak) for heavier compaction and skipping. The trade-off: options 1 and 2 add slight per-batch write latency; scheduled OPTIMIZE acquires a short table lock that can delay concurrent reads. On tables with Liquid Clustering, OPTIMIZE automatically clusters data incrementally — no ZORDER needed. Use DESCRIBE HISTORY to see file counts per version and verify improvement.
fj: I'd run a daily OPTIMIZE job to compact the small files.
fs: A scheduled OPTIMIZE is a good fallback, but it adds operational overhead and a brief table lock. The idiomatic Databricks fix is enabling autoOptimize.optimizeWrite and autoOptimize.autoCompact as table properties — they compact continuously at write time without a separate job.

code.label: Enable auto-compaction on the Delta table
lines: -- numFiles column shows files per version

followups: What is the difference between optimizeWrite and autoCompact in terms of when compaction happens?
